prompt: Marketing edits our notification-email templates by hand, and the current renderer is a pile of str.replace calls that fails silently and ships '{{ user.nmae }}' to customers. Replace it with a real (tiny) compiler: `microtmpl.py` with compile_template(source) returning a render(context) callable, plus TemplateSyntaxError (with .line and .col ints, 1-based, pointing at the opening braces of the offending tag) and RenderError (with .path naming the value that was missing). Tags look like {{ user.name }} or {{ title | upper }} or {{ desc | trunc:40 }}; whitespace inside the braces is flexible; everything outside tags (including lone { or } and stray }}) passes through untouched. Paths are dot-separated; an all-digits segment indexes into a list, but against a dict it's just a string key (we have year-keyed dicts like stats.2026.total, don't break those). Filters chain left to right: upper, lower, title, trunc:N (keep the first N characters), default:VALUE. Compile time must catch EVERY malformed tag before anything renders — unclosed tag, empty tag, unknown filter, trunc missing or non-integer arg, an arg handed to upper/lower/title, bare default with no colon — all TemplateSyntaxError with accurate line/col; marketing needs the exact spot. At render time a path that's missing (absent key, index out of range, traversing into a scalar) or that resolves to None raises RenderError — UNLESS the chain contains default:VALUE, in which case filters before default are skipped for the missing value, default supplies its arg, and filters after it still apply ({{ nick | default:anon | upper }} -> 'ANON'). Non-string values render via str(). The returned render must be reusable across many contexts with zero shared state. test_microtmpl.py is the contract: `python3 test_microtmpl.py` must pass, hands off the test file.
